rows.v: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea — the 'who' or 'what' of a sentence.
Nouns can be common (dog) or proper (Rex), concrete (table) or abstract (freedom), singular or plural. They can be subjects, objects, or complements.
Don't capitalize common nouns; only proper nouns (specific names) get a capital letter.
The teacher gave the students hope. (teacher, students, hope are nouns)
A word that stands in for a noun so you don't have to repeat it — he, she, it, they, this, who.
Pronouns replace a noun (the antecedent) and must match it in number and gender. Types include personal, possessive, relative, and demonstrative.
Make sure it's always clear which noun a pronoun refers to — vague pronouns confuse readers.
Maria lost her keys, so she called a locksmith. (her, she replace Maria)
A word that expresses an action or a state of being — the engine of every sentence.
Every complete sentence needs a verb. Action verbs (run, think) show what the subject does; linking verbs (is, seem) connect it to a description.
Don't confuse action verbs with linking verbs — 'She is happy' uses a linking verb, not an action.
Birds fly. The soup tastes salty. (fly = action, tastes = linking)
A word that describes or modifies a noun — telling which, what kind, or how many.
Adjectives answer 'what kind?', 'which one?', or 'how many?' They usually sit before the noun or after a linking verb.
Use commas between adjectives only if you could swap their order or insert 'and' (a tall, dark stranger).
The red car. Seven loud dogs. The sky is blue. (red, seven, loud, blue)
A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb — telling how, when, where, or to what degree.
Many adverbs end in -ly (quickly), but not all (very, often, here). They add detail about manner, time, place, or intensity.
Avoid piling on adverbs; a strong verb usually beats a weak verb plus an adverb ('sprinted' over 'ran quickly').
She sang beautifully. He arrived late. It's very cold. (beautifully, late, very)
A word that shows the relationship between a noun and other words — usually position, direction, or time.
Prepositions (in, on, at, under, before, between) introduce a phrase that links a noun to the rest of the sentence.
The old 'never end a sentence with a preposition' rule is a myth — natural English often does ('What are you waiting for?').
The cat sat on the mat under the table. (on, under)
A word that joins words, phrases, or clauses together — and, but, or, because, although.
Coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) join equals; subordinating ones (because, while) attach a dependent clause.
Use a comma before a coordinating conjunction only when it joins two complete sentences.
Tea or coffee? I stayed because it rained. (or, because)
A short word or phrase that expresses sudden emotion — Wow! Ouch! Oh no!
Interjections stand apart from the grammar of a sentence and are usually followed by an exclamation point or comma.
Use them sparingly in formal writing; they're best for dialogue and casual tone.
Wow, that's amazing! Ouch! Well, I suppose so. (Wow, Ouch, Well)
The small words a, an, and the that introduce a noun and signal whether it's specific or general.
'The' is definite (a specific thing); 'a/an' is indefinite (any one). Use 'an' before a vowel sound, 'a' before a consonant sound.
It's 'an hour' (silent h, vowel sound) but 'a university' (sounds like 'yoo') — go by sound, not spelling.
The dog chased a cat for an hour. (the, a, an)
The verb form for actions happening now or that happen habitually.
Simple present states facts and habits (I walk); it also covers general truths. Third-person singular usually adds -s (he walks).
Don't forget the -s on he/she/it ('she runs', not 'she run') — a very common error.
I work downtown. The sun rises in the east. She walks to school.
The verb form for actions that already happened and are now complete.
Regular verbs add -ed (walked); irregular verbs change form unpredictably (go→went, see→saw). It places the action firmly in the past.
Irregular past forms must be memorized — 'I seen it' is wrong; it's 'I saw it' or 'I have seen it'.
She walked home. They went to the beach. I saw the movie.
The verb form for actions that haven't happened yet.
English forms the future with 'will' + verb, or with 'going to' for plans and intentions. There's no single future ending.
'Will' and 'going to' differ subtly: 'going to' suits planned intentions, 'will' suits spontaneous decisions and predictions.
I will call you. We are going to travel next year.
An aspect that links a past action to another time — using 'have/has/had' plus a past participle.
Present perfect (have done) connects past to now; past perfect (had done) shows one past action before another. It's about completion relative to a reference point.
Use present perfect for unfinished time ('I have lived here for years'), simple past for finished time ('I lived there in 2010').
I have finished. She had left before we arrived.
An aspect showing an action in progress — using 'be' plus the -ing form.
The progressive (am/is/are/was + verb-ing) emphasizes that an action is ongoing rather than complete.
Some 'state' verbs resist the progressive — say 'I know', not 'I am knowing'.
I am reading. They were sleeping. She will be waiting.
Two ways to arrange a sentence: active (subject acts) or passive (subject is acted upon).
Active: 'The dog bit the man.' Passive: 'The man was bitten by the dog.' Passive uses 'be' + past participle and can drop the doer.
Prefer active voice for clarity and energy; use passive deliberately when the doer is unknown or unimportant.
The committee made the decision. (active) / The decision was made. (passive)
The form of a verb that signals whether something is a fact, a command, or a hypothetical.
Indicative states facts (You are here); imperative gives commands (Be here); subjunctive expresses wishes or unreal conditions (If I were you).
The subjunctive 'were' survives in set phrases — 'If I were you', not 'If I was you', in formal usage.
She is ready. (indicative) Be ready! (imperative) I wish I were ready. (subjunctive)
The two essential halves of a sentence: the subject (who/what) and the predicate (what's said about it).
The subject is the noun or pronoun doing or being something; the predicate contains the verb and everything that completes the thought.
Every complete sentence needs both — a string of words with no subject-predicate pair is a fragment.
The tired dog | slept all afternoon. (subject | predicate)
A group of words containing a subject and a verb — the building block of sentences.
An independent clause stands alone as a sentence; a dependent (subordinate) clause cannot, because it begins with a word like 'because' or 'when'.
A dependent clause left on its own is a fragment — 'Because it rained.' needs a main clause to complete it.
She smiled (independent) / because she won (dependent).
A group of related words that lacks a subject-verb pair, acting as a single unit.
Phrases (prepositional, noun, verb, participial) add information but can't stand alone as sentences because they have no subject doing a verb.
Don't mistake a long phrase for a sentence — 'Running down the street' is a phrase, not a complete thought.
in the morning / the big red barn / running quickly (all phrases)
A simple sentence has one independent clause; a compound has two or more joined as equals.
Simple: one complete thought. Compound: two complete thoughts linked by a comma + conjunction, or a semicolon.
Join two independent clauses with a comma AND a conjunction — a comma alone creates a comma splice.
I ran. (simple) / I ran, and she walked. (compound)
A sentence with one independent clause plus at least one dependent clause.
It pairs a complete thought with a subordinate one introduced by a word like because, although, when, or if.
When the dependent clause comes first, put a comma after it; when it comes second, usually don't.
Although it rained, we went out. / We went out although it rained.
An incomplete sentence missing a subject, a verb, or a complete thought.
Fragments are pieces punctuated as if they were full sentences but can't stand alone grammatically.
Fix a fragment by attaching it to a nearby sentence or adding the missing subject or verb.
Fragment: 'Running to the store.' Fix: 'She was running to the store.'
The rule that a verb must match its subject in number — singular subject, singular verb.
Singular subjects take singular verbs (The dog barks); plural subjects take plural verbs (The dogs bark).
Watch for phrases between subject and verb — 'The box of chocolates IS', not 'are', because the subject is 'box'.
The list of items is long. The students are ready.
The rule that a pronoun must agree with the noun it replaces in number and gender.
A singular antecedent needs a singular pronoun; a plural one needs a plural pronoun. The pronoun should clearly point to one noun.
Singular 'they' is now widely accepted for an unknown or nonbinary person ('Each student brought their book').
The players grabbed their gear. A nurse knows their patients.
Whether to use a subject pronoun (I, he, she, they) or object pronoun (me, him, her, them).
Subject pronouns do the action; object pronouns receive it. Test tricky pairs by dropping the other person.
'Between you and me', never 'between you and I' — after a preposition you need the object form.
She and I left. (subjects) / He gave it to her and me. (objects)
The principle that items in a series or comparison should share the same grammatical form.
List items, paired ideas, and comparisons read smoothly when they match: all nouns, all -ing verbs, all the same structure.
Faulty: 'I like hiking, swimming, and to read.' Parallel: 'hiking, swimming, and reading.'
She is smart, kind, and generous. We came to see, to learn, and to grow.
The dot that ends a statement or command — the most basic full stop.
Use a period to close a declarative or imperative sentence, and in many abbreviations (Dr., etc.).
Don't use a period to join two sentences into one — that needs a conjunction, semicolon, or staying separate.
The meeting is over. Please sit down.
The mark that ends a direct question.
Place it at the end of a direct question. Don't use it for indirect questions, which are really statements.
Indirect questions take a period, not a question mark — 'I wonder what time it is.', not '...is?'
Direct: 'What time is it?' Indirect: 'She asked what time it was.'
The mark that signals strong emotion, emphasis, or a forceful command.
It ends an exclamatory sentence or a sharp command, conveying surprise, excitement, or urgency.
Use it sparingly — overusing exclamation points drains their force and looks unprofessional.
Watch out! That's incredible! Stop right now!
The most versatile mark — it separates, groups, and clarifies within a sentence.
Commas separate list items, set off introductory and nonessential elements, and join clauses with a conjunction.
A comma alone can't join two sentences (that's a comma splice) — add a conjunction or use a semicolon.
We bought apples, pears, and figs. After lunch, we left.
A mark that links two closely related complete sentences, or separates complex list items.
Use a semicolon to join two independent clauses without a conjunction, or to separate list items that already contain commas.
Both sides of a semicolon must be able to stand alone as sentences — otherwise use a comma.
I came; I saw; I conquered. We visited Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Bern.
A mark that introduces a list, an explanation, or a quotation — it points forward.
A colon follows a complete sentence and announces what comes next: a list, a definition, or an elaboration.
Use a complete sentence before a colon — not 'The colors are: red, blue' but 'I bought three colors: red, blue, green.'
She had one goal: to win. Bring these: a pen, paper, and ID.
A versatile mark — the em dash — that adds emphasis, interruption, or an aside.
An em dash sets off a strong break, an abrupt change, or a parenthetical you want to spotlight rather than tuck away.
Don't overuse dashes; too many make writing feel breathless. They're stronger than commas, looser than colons.
He grabbed the keys — and then froze. The plan — risky as it was — worked.
Curved marks that enclose extra, non-essential information or asides.
Parentheses tuck in a clarification, example, or aside that could be removed without breaking the sentence.
Keep parenthetical asides brief; if the info is essential, it probably shouldn't be in parentheses.
The trip (our first abroad) was unforgettable. Call me (after 5 pm).
An error where two complete sentences are wrongly joined by only a comma.
A comma splice happens when a comma alone connects two independent clauses, which it can't do on its own.
Fix it four ways: add a conjunction, swap in a semicolon, use a period, or subordinate one clause.
Wrong: 'It rained, we stayed home.' Right: 'It rained, so we stayed home.'
A mark that shows possession or marks the missing letters in a contraction.
Add 's for singular possession (dog's bone), just ' for plural possession (dogs' park), and use it for contractions (can't = cannot).
Never use an apostrophe to make a plain plural — it's 'apples', not 'apple's'.
The cat's toy. The girls' team. It's late (it is). Don't go.
Marks that enclose direct speech, quoted text, or certain titles.
Use quotation marks around someone's exact words and around titles of short works. Periods and commas go inside them in US style.
Don't use quotation marks for emphasis — that 'fresh' sign suggests the food is anything but.
She said, "Hello." The story is titled "The Gift."
A short mark that joins words or parts of words into a single unit.
Hyphens link compound modifiers before a noun (well-known author), join some prefixes, and split words at line ends.
Don't confuse the short hyphen with the longer dash — the hyphen joins, the dash separates.
A well-known writer. Twenty-one. A state-of-the-art lab.
Three spaced dots that mark omitted words or a trailing-off thought.
An ellipsis (…) shows that text has been left out of a quotation, or signals a pause or unfinished thought.
Don't scatter ellipses through casual writing for effect — they read as vague or hesitant.
She quoted, "We the people … form a union." I wonder…
Square marks that enclose editorial additions inside quoted material.
Square brackets [ ] mark words you insert into a quotation for clarity, distinct from the original speaker's words.
Don't mix up square brackets with parentheses — brackets signal an editor's insertion, not the author's aside.
He said, "[The plan] worked perfectly." (clarifying who/what)
The classic mix-up: 'its' (belonging to it) versus 'it's' (it is / it has).
'Its' is the possessive (the dog wagged its tail); 'it's' is always a contraction of 'it is' or 'it has'.
Test it: if you can replace it with 'it is', use 'it's'. If not, use 'its'.
The cat licked its paws. It's raining. It's been a long day.
'Your' (belonging to you) versus 'you're' (you are).
'Your' shows possession; 'you're' is the contraction of 'you are'. They sound identical but mean different things.
Test it: if 'you are' fits, write 'you're'. Otherwise it's 'your'.
Your book is here. You're going to love it.
The three sound-alikes: there (place), their (possession), they're (they are).
'There' points to a place or fact; 'their' shows possession; 'they're' means 'they are'.
Swap-test: 'they are' → they're; belongs to them → their; everything else → there.
They're putting their bags over there.
'Affect' (usually a verb, to influence) versus 'effect' (usually a noun, a result).
Affect is normally the action ('the rain affected traffic'); effect is normally the result ('the effect was chaos').
Trick: A=Action (affect), E=End result (effect). The rare exceptions ('effect change') confuse even pros.
The news affected her. The effect was immediate.
'Who' (subject) versus 'whom' (object) — the formal distinction many people skip.
Use 'who' when it's doing the action, 'whom' when it's receiving it. 'Whom' follows prepositions in formal use.
Test: answer with he/him — 'he' → who, 'him' → whom. ('To whom?' → 'to him.')
Who called? (he called) / To whom did you give it? (to him)
Two or more complete sentences crammed together without proper punctuation.
A run-on (or fused sentence) joins independent clauses with nothing between them, leaving the reader breathless.
Fix by splitting into separate sentences, or joining with a semicolon or a comma + conjunction.
Wrong: 'I ran I was late.' Right: 'I ran because I was late.'
A descriptive phrase that accidentally attaches to the wrong subject — often unintentionally funny.
A modifier 'dangles' when the thing it's supposed to describe isn't actually in the sentence, so it lands on the wrong noun.
Make sure the word right after an opening modifier is the thing it describes.
Wrong: 'Walking home, the rain started.' (the rain wasn't walking) Right: 'Walking home, I felt the rain start.'
